essential_question: Can you find a real plane and sort real objects into a union and an intersection?
objective: Identify a real-world plane and name it using three non-collinear points, and physically sort a group of objects into the union and intersection of two overlapping sets.

content.heading: From definitions to real loops
bullets: Yesterday: defined planes, sets, union, intersection, subset, and the null set.
Today: hunt for a real plane and sort real objects into two overlapping sets.
The test: can you place an object in the RIGHT region — and defend why?

content.heading: Gather + choose categories
content.note: Open the project card. Pick your flat surface (the plane), your two loops, and two categories likely to overlap (e.g., round ∩ metal). Gather 10+ small objects before you start sorting.

content.heading: Lay it out, then sort
bullets: Lay two overlapping loops (string or hoops) on your plane — label them Set A and Set B.
Sort every object into A only, B only, both (the overlap), or outside both loops.
Mark three points on your plane that are NOT all on one line, and name the plane using them.

content.preview: Session 3: defend why two planes always meet in a line, then the checkpoint.

success_criteria.text: I can identify a real plane and name it using three non-collinear points
I can sort real objects into the union and intersection of two overlapping sets
I can explain what physically sorting taught me that the definitions alone didn't

questions.prompt: List everything in your Set A ∪ B (everything inside either loop) and everything in your Set A ∩ B (only the overlap).
questions.answer: Any honest list matching the student's own sorted objects — e.g., A ∪ B: {coin, spoon, marble, ring}; A ∩ B: {marble} if 'marble' is both round AND metal.
questions.explain: Rubric: the union lists every object inside either loop with no double-counting (1 pt); the intersection lists only objects that landed in the shared overlap zone (1 pt).

questions.prompt: True or false: an object sitting outside BOTH loops is still part of A ∪ B.
questions.explain: A ∪ B only includes what's inside at least one of the loops; anything outside both loops is outside the union entirely.

questions.prompt: Mark three points on your plane that are NOT all on one line, and write the plane's name using them.
questions.answer: Any three non-collinear points on the chosen surface, correctly written as a plane name (e.g., 'plane RST').
questions.explain: Three non-collinear points always pin down exactly one plane; if all three were collinear, they would not be enough to name a unique flat surface.

questions.prompt: Reflect: which was trickier — deciding whether an object belonged in the overlap, or picking three non-collinear points for your plane? What did the hands-on sorting teach you that the definitions alone didn't?
